projects.current.overview.title: site.br
site.hopen
Progress notes
site.hclose
site.br
projects.current.overview.1: A Slackware64-current (2026-05-01) build environment was created to compile the software components required for the HA cluster reference architecture.
projects.current.overview.2: A Slackware64-current gold base image was built.
projects.current.overview.3: Corosync, Pacemaker, DRBD and dependencies were packaged, as source, from git repos then built using modified scripts from the
Code was compiled and packaged so that it can later be installed into a gold image from which qcow2 snapshots would be created for each node.
projects.current.overview.4: A Slackware64-current gold base image with HA components was built. This is the base image from which the .
two cluster nodes were built. The cluster images were built as snapshots and the configuration was captured
so that ansible code can be written once the config is determined and proven to be operationally correct.
projects.current.overview.5: The images were pulled into my Virtualisation platform, covert (Chris' Own Virtualisation Environment for Research and Testing) and the qcow images were
created for the two member nodes, dee, and dum. The two cluster nodes were named dee and dum, collectively forming the Tweedle cluster. A
witness/quorum node is being considered and will likely be named alice
projects.current.overview.6: DRBD has been configured with a private network. Disks were synchronised and failover tests independent of pacemaker/corosync. In an HA pair, DRBD volumes
need to be set up and then managed by corosync.

A cluster Emerges...
site.hclose
projects.current.discussion.title: site.br
site.hopen
Discussion
site.hclose
site.br
projects.current.discussion.1: DRBD, Corosync and Pacemaker were successfully built and integrated to the point where cluster quorum, failover and storage replication could be demonstrated
independently. A timing-related issue affecting stable operation during failback remains unresolved. Because the current implementation is hosted entirely within
a virtual environment sharing common physical storage, further investigation has been deferred pending access to additional physical hardware. Focus has now
switched to building a SLURM based reference cluster with Singularity/Apptainer containers as functional units to be deployed by SLURM. This is reminiscent of
the Cray Urika-CS methodology. A key focus will be multi-tenancy, fast deployment, and simple management for scientific workloads.
projects.current.discussion.2: The resulting environment naturally separates into several independent architectural planes.
projects.current.discussion.3: The Infrastructure Plane provides storage, virtualisation, image
management and deployment services. This functionality is primarily provided by COVERT. The Identity Plane provides authentication, authorisation and service
identity through OpenLDAP, MIT Kerberos5 and supporting infrastructure. The Workload Plane contains the actual reference architectures, including high-availability
clusters, Hadoop environments and scientific computing clusters. The Data Plane remains largely independent of the workloads themselves. Applications interact with
data through filesystem access and associated permissions rather than through application-specific security mechanisms.
This separation allows workloads to be rebuilt, replaced or upgraded without requiring significant changes to either the identity infrastructure or the underlying
projects.current.discussion.4: The vision was greater, far, than either time, or resources permitted. That's not a bad thing — the design has been reasonably stable aver the past decade with minor
changes being introduced when augmented functionality was required to support a new scenario. I'd intended, at some point, to be able to store definitions for images,
and to be able to call Qemu or Singularity (now Apptainer) as required. An important reason behind my resistance to migraton of my system to KVM was that I had non-intel
architectures that I needed to support — these being ARM, NIOS-2, Pacoblaze, and i386. In one of my prior roles, I often assisted users in running non-intel images
up as part of their work. Being able to prototype their use-case for them was a valuable service that I was able to provide.
projects.current.discussion.5: Some important considerations arose during my attempt to conceptualise my system make it simpler. The security model is implemented as a foundational service rather
than as an application feature. In this respect it bears some resemblance to aspects of the Cray Urika-GX architecture. The resulting scientific cluster architecture
is more reminiscent of a Cray Urika-CS, especially in terms of it being Slurm-centric, and depending on singularity containers. Management and metadata storage have been
abstracted into one functional unit. Security and identity has been abstracted into another plane — and not just for users, but for the hosts as well. Each
system is built on a qcow2 image that is derived from a gold image. Each platform is built around some type of cluster solution (eg. Corosync, Hadoop, or traditional
projects.current.discussion.6: The security model is built from the bottom up and resides outside any of the architectures. It is built on OpenLDAP, MIT Kerberos5, and makes use of Cyrus-SASL and
file/directory-level ACLs. The Scientific Computing Reference Architecture has been designed with multi-tenancy in mind. A central design principle is the separation
of computation, scheduling, metadata and data. The scheduler manages work. Containers provide application environments. Identity services determine authorisation.
Storage systems provide persistence. No individual component owns the entire workflow. The Identity and Authorisation components define what operations can be performed
upon the data, and by whom. The programs and Singularity containers are separate, though, there is a use-case where acess to the actual programs might need to be
restricted — and this is possible, too.
projects.current.discussion.7: I harp on security because it's difficult to 'bolt on later' but it is essential. I'm also very conscious of file ownership/permissions. It was not uncommon to encounter
users who were not adept at dealing with unix file ownership. In fact, I had a discussion, on one occasion, with a group of veteran admins who did not have an appreciation for
different ownership semantics, nor for the implementation-specific variation in these. It becomes even more of a concern when changes occur, people leave an organisation, and
files are 'orphaned', in terms of ownership, if their credentials are deleted from the Identity management system. This is a serious concern and one that I have given a lot of
thought to over the years. Ultimately, the problem being addressed is not merely authentication or authorisation, but provenance. Provenance must be recorded automatically
wherever possible. Systems that rely upon users to manually maintain records of execution, ownership or data lineage will eventually fail because people change roles, move
organisations, retire, or simply make mistakes. Scientific and industrial computing environments must be capable of demonstrating who performed a task, what data was used,
what outputs were produced, and under which authority the work was executed. Patents, ownership of IP, claims of invention, litigation, valuation, National Security,
evidentiary claims in terms of exculpatory evidence are all potentially riding on provenance. It's not a trivial problem — but it could be made more so.
